topic: Someone finds out their medical records were mixed up with another patient's
raw_output:
[story 1]

The file landed on my desk with a soft thud, the crisp paper feeling strangely heavy in my hands. It was my medical history, but the name at the top was wrong. It belonged to someone else entirely. My breath hitched, a shallow catch in my chest. I scanned the pages, my eyes skipping over familiar diagnoses until I found the jarring discrepancy: a recent surgery date, a specific allergy notation that wasn't mine. A cold prickle traced its way down my spine. I reread the section detailing my past treatments, the details suddenly feeling alien, like someone else’s private diary had been slipped under my door. I started tapping my pen rapidly against the wood of the desk, the small, sharp sounds echoing in the quiet office. Every glance toward the door felt like being watched.

[story 2]

I stared at the printout under the harsh fluorescent light, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the folder. The discrepancy was glaring—a blood type listed that simply didn't match mine, a prescription for medication I’d never taken. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room tilt slightly. I pushed the papers away as if they were suddenly radioactive, my hands trembling so badly I had to consciously stop myself from shaking them harder. I walked to the window, pulling the blinds shut, trying to force the outside world into a manageable shape. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I kept replaying the moment I’d found the file, a loop of suspicion tightening in my stomach, wondering who had orchestrated this clumsy, invasive mistake.

[story 3]

When Dr. Evans handed over the chart, I felt a strange paralysis set in. The layout was correct, the handwriting familiar, yet the details—the precise dates, the lab results—were all misaligned. I traced a line with my finger, my gaze snagging on a section describing a condition I had no recollection of. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I looked up quickly, expecting to see someone standing behind me, but the hallway was empty. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth alarming. I quickly tucked the documents into my bag, moving with an exaggerated slowness toward the exit, acutely aware of the weight of the information clutched in my mind. The sound of my own footsteps seemed overly loud on the linoleum floor.
